title: Guest Speaker Ken Newberry on Architecture, Collaboration, and Building Better Client Relationships
summary: Architect Ken Newberry of Newberry Architecture joined the Builder's Edge group to share 35+ years of experience in residential design and construction. The session covered topics including budget alignment, architect-contractor collaboration, drawing detail expectations, project documentation, and how contractors can build relationships with architects. Participants asked candid questions about real challenges they face working with architects, and Ken offered frank, experience-based guidance throughout.

teachings.explanation: Ken's foundational philosophy is to always hire people who are better at a given task than he is. He applied this to contracting after trying it himself, and later to scheduling and team management. This mindset reduces ego-driven conflict and builds stronger project outcomes.
teachings.quote: I always believe in hiring people that are better doing things than I am.

teachings.concept: Budget Conversation Must Happen on Day One
teachings.explanation: Architects and contractors should establish the client's realistic budget at the very first meeting, before any design work begins. If the stated budget does not match the client's vision, that gap must be addressed immediately through honest conversation and preliminary pricing rather than letting it surface after costly drawings are completed.
teachings.quote: One of the questions that I ask really quickly day one is what's your budget. And if someone tells me they want to spend five hundred thousand and I'm hearing what they want to do and I know in my heart that it is two million, I let them go on and then I say okay so let's talk about budget and reality of budget.

teachings.explanation: The first step in any project should be creating a written project program — a document that captures everything the client wants, including imagery, site details, and budget. This program becomes the single source of truth before any design begins and helps prevent scope and budget misalignment later.
teachings.quote: Our first step in any project is to create a program, what we call a project program, and it is a description of the house and the site and everything. It spells everything out and that's the most important part.

teachings.explanation: The adversarial dynamic that often exists between architects and contractors is counterproductive. When both parties collaborate as a team — with the shared goal of a seamless client experience — projects run better and generate more referrals for everyone involved.
teachings.quote: As far as I'm concerned, when we finish a project the client shouldn't know there were any problems at all. We did our job. We collaborated with the contractor and did a great job.

teachings.concept: Documentation as the Strongest Accountability Tool
teachings.explanation: When holding architects, contractors, or any project partner accountable to deadlines, thorough documentation of all commitments is the most powerful tool. Recording what was promised, when, and in what meeting creates a clear record that protects all parties and drives follow-through.
teachings.quote: The person that wins that battle has the best documentation. You told me on June 10th that on July 15th you would have these ready and we had a meeting at three o'clock in the afternoon on June 10th and you said this.

teachings.concept: Proactive Deadline Management — Don't Wait Until the Deadline
teachings.explanation: Professionals should check in repeatedly leading up to any deadline rather than discovering a miss on the due date. Regularly asking 'are you on schedule, do you need anything from me' surfaces problems early enough to adjust, and communicates accountability without hostility.
teachings.quote: Don't wait until the deadline to find out you're not going to meet the deadline. I tell my team that all the time. Ask five times between now and then: are you still on schedule, do you need anything from me?

teachings.concept: Scope of Drawings Determines Who Makes Decisions
teachings.explanation: When architectural drawings are minimal or under-detailed, someone still has to make the decisions those drawings would have resolved. That responsibility falls to the interior designer, the client, or worst case the subcontractors. Understanding this helps all parties set appropriate expectations and budget for the right level of service.
teachings.quote: Someone is going to have to make these decisions. Do you want the plumber to make the decision? Do you want the cabinet guy to make the decision? Someone has to make those decisions.

teachings.concept: 3D Modeling as a Differentiator for Contractors
teachings.explanation: Contractors who learn 3D modeling gain a significant competitive advantage. They can identify conflicts between structural engineering and architectural drawings before construction begins, communicate more effectively with architects and clients, and position themselves as high-value collaborators rather than just builders.
teachings.quote: If you learn 3D modeling you're going to figure out — especially if you're a 3D thinker already — conflicts like a glue lam below a toilet or lighting or air conditioning ducts. Architects will love you if you know how to do 3D modeling.

teachings.concept: Get Your Foot in the Door with Smaller Projects
teachings.explanation: Building relationships with architects often starts by taking on a smaller or less desirable project that gives both parties a chance to test the relationship. Once trust is established through a smaller job, larger and more complex projects naturally follow.
teachings.quote: Be willing to take on a smaller project because we do that with new contractors — let's test them out on something fairly simple. Get your foot in the door. Step one, you've done it great.

teachings.explanation: When architects, contractors, and designers align privately on strategy before a client meeting, they can present solutions as a unified front. This prevents clients from playing parties against each other and creates a more authoritative, trustworthy experience.
teachings.quote: There's strategy and prep behind this. It's not always just show up. There is certainly strategy and prep before a meeting if we're trying to reach a certain understanding where everybody comes together and gets on the same page.

actionable_advice: Ask the client about their budget on day one of any project meeting, before any design work begins.
Pull in a trusted contractor early in the design process to provide preliminary pricing after schematic design, before committing to full construction documents.
Document all commitments — dates, deadlines, and what was promised — during every project meeting to maintain accountability.
Follow up repeatedly before any deadline, asking partners if they are on schedule and whether they need anything, rather than waiting until the due date.
Make sure all key project parties — architect, contractor, interior designer — are copied on communications so no one is making decisions in silos.
Hold a strategy session with your collaborators before client meetings to align on a unified position, especially when setting or resetting expectations.
Contractors looking to attract better architects should learn 3D modeling rather than basic drafting, as it adds immediate, visible value on complex projects.
If you want to build a relationship with an architect, be willing to take on a smaller or difficult project first to prove your capabilities and earn trust.
Take the names and identifying information off architectural drawings before sharing them with others to protect client privacy.
When working with an architect who produces minimal drawings, have an explicit conversation upfront about who will be responsible for making on-site decisions that the drawings don't address.
Contractors should reach out to architects for lunch meetings and send portfolios of their work to get introductions and build referral relationships.

Check with local counties about whether an architect's stamp is actually required for residential projects, as in many states designers can legally do the work, which broadens the pool of potential collaborators.
client_challenges: A neighbor hired a drafter instead of a licensed architect, and the plans came back far over budget, requiring the entire second story to be removed — raising the question of how to keep design and budget aligned from the start.

A builder in Dallas is working with a well-known architect whose standard plan sets lack detail, leaving decisions to be made by subcontractors, and wants to know how to address this without damaging a new relationship.
A builder discovered it is not legally permissible — even with permission from the original architect — for a second architect to modify or work from another architect's drawings.
A contractor is experiencing repeated missed deadlines from an architect who overpromises delivery dates, with no clear communication about why timelines are slipping.

A residential design and remodel company in South Carolina cannot find architects willing to collaborate with them — firms either refuse remodels entirely, won't communicate with the contractor, or won't meet jointly with the client.

A contractor wants to understand what level of drawing detail they should expect to receive at different stages of a project, and whether it is reasonable to request a sample set of plans for reference.
A contractor in Houston wants to develop drafting and 3D modeling skills and is looking for guidance on which software to learn and where to find in-person training.

An interior designer and builder on a project are experiencing delays caused by unclear dependencies — the designer cannot make decisions without input from the architect, but it is unclear what exactly the architect still owes.
notable_quotes: On the client experience: 'I overheard the client say we never had one problem on our project the entire time — it took two years to build — and we never had any problems. And I was like, we did our job.'

On knowing when a contractor isn't the right fit: 'I know enough to know this guy or this woman is in trouble. This contractor is in trouble here because they will never be able to build the house that this client wants for what they're thinking.'

On life being too short for difficult clients: 'I prefer working with really nice clients and people. I life too short to put my team through a horrible client situation — if we have a yeller and a screamer I'm like, that's not how we roll.'
On the value of contractors: 'Hats off to any contractors on here. That takes a special personality and patience. I tried it and you guys do it a lot better than I do.'

On client privacy with drawings: 'We had people in and we'd be pulling up drawings to show people and they go, oh we didn't know you did their house. The next thing we know our client's getting a phone call. So we just took all names off of projects 15 years ago.'

On AI rendering: 'You really do have to watch it because if you do AI and then you go to blow it up on a big screen in front of a client and you haven't checked it, you'll be surprised at what AI thought was a good idea.'
